claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can go through each child separately.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex - he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely to meet you! Alex, at 8 years old, falls into the "under 16" category, which is great news as children under 16 automatically qualify as a "qualifying child" for Child Benefit purposes.

Let me continue with the next step of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex is only 8 years old, he wouldn't be working 24 or more hours per week, so I'll proceed with "yes" for this check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, I need to know if he receives Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name. 

At 8 years old, this would be very unusual, but I just need to confirm - is Alex receiving any of these benefits himself? (Yes, he receives these benefits / No, he does not)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, that confirms Alex isn't receiving benefits in his own right.

**Next question: Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

At 8 years old, I would expect the answer is no, but just to confirm - is Alex undertaking any kind of apprenticeship programme?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's just in primary school at his age.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - Alex is just at primary school, so no apprenticeship concerns there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme AND you're:
   - Working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months), OR
   - Actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days), OR
   - Have sufficient resources to support yourself and family (and not claiming certain benefits), OR
   - Studying with sufficient resources, OR
   - A family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
4. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next, I need to confirm you are responsible for Alex.**

You qualify as "responsible" if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - this means paying at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me, but I do help support him. I contribute towards things like his clothes and other bits he needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since you contribute towards Alex's upkeep (clothes and other necessities), that can count as being "responsible" for him - as long as your contributions are at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Just to confirm - **are your contributions towards Alex's care at least equivalent to the Child Benefit payment amount?** 

This would be roughly equivalent to the weekly Child Benefit rate (you can check the current rates at gov.uk). Your contributions can include things like clothes, food, pocket money, presents, or contributions to housing - and can be weekly, monthly, or even lump sum payments.

Would you say your contributions meet or exceed this amount?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'd say so. I give about £30 a week towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful to know. £30 per week would certainly exceed the Child Benefit amount, so you qualify as being responsible for Alex through your contributions to his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Since Alex doesn't live with you, it's possible that the person he lives with (perhaps his other parent or guardian?) may already be claiming Child Benefit for him.

- **No one else is claiming** Child Benefit for Alex
- **Someone else is claiming**, but they have agreed to transfer the claim to you
- **Someone else is claiming**, and there's a dispute about who should receive it

Which of these applies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know. Now I need to check Alex's current living situation.

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- **In hospital** (for an extended stay)
- **In local authority care**
- **With foster parents**
- **Normal living arrangement** (e.g., with family, at home with the other parent, etc.)

Which of these best describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's in a normal living arrangement - he lives with his mum.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

For example, is Alex receiving any local authority funding for his housing or care costs?

- **No**, the council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance
- **Yes**, the council is contributing to his accommodation or maintenance

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! That completes the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

### Summary:
- ✅ **Alex qualifies** as a "qualifying child" (he's under 16)
- ✅ **You meet UK residency requirements** (living in Manchester)
- ✅ **You're responsible for Alex** through your financial contributions (£30/week towards his upkeep)
- ✅ **No one else is claiming** Child Benefit for Alex
- ✅ **No local authority funding** is involved

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note:
If you or your partner earns over **£60,000**, be aware of the **High Income Child Benefit Charge** - this may affect how much you keep, but it doesn't affect your eligibility to claim.

📎 **More information and how to apply:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he automatically qualifies as a 'qualifying child'. He is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. His parent (the claimant) normally lives in the UK (Manchester), is responsible for Alex through contributing approximately £30 per week towards his upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount), no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, he is in a normal living arrangement (with his mum), and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their son Alex. Although Alex doesn't live with the claimant, the claimant qualifies as 'responsible' for Alex through their financial contributions (£30/week) towards his upkeep, and no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for him.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he automatically qualifies as a 'qualifying child'. He is not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. His parent (the claimant) normally lives in the UK (Manchester), is responsible for Alex through contributing approximately £30 per week towards his upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount), no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, he is in a normal living arrangement (with his mum), and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their son Alex. Although Alex doesn't live with the claimant, the claimant qualifies as 'responsible' for Alex through their financial contributions (£30/week) towards his upkeep, and no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for him.
